title: SEGMENT 6 — Worms that live twice as long: daf-2, age-1, and the genetics of aging

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Worms that live twice as long: daf-2, age-1, and the genetics of aging
For most of the twentieth century, biologists had a tacit assumption about aging.
They assumed it was, basically, an accumulation of damage.
Random wear and tear.
Mutations piling up in your DNA.
Oxidized proteins.
Misfolded aggregates.
Telomere shortening.
A general slow decay of cellular function that there wasn't, in any meaningful sense, a program for.
You aged because you wore out, the same way a car wears out from being driven.
In the early 1990s, a researcher named Cynthia Kenyon, working at UCSF, did an experiment in worms that shattered that assumption.
Her question was simple: could you find single-gene mutations that made worms live longer?
The conventional wisdom said no — aging was systemic, distributed, not the kind of thing a single gene could control.
But Kenyon ran the screen anyway.
She mutagenized worms and looked for ones that died later than normal.
Wild-type worms live about two to three weeks at 20 degrees Celsius.
That's their natural lifespan.
Kenyon screened for worms still alive at three or four weeks — the kind of age at which essentially all wild-type worms are dead.
And she found them.
Mutations in a gene called **daf-2** — a daf gene we mentioned in the last segment, related to dauer formation — produced worms that lived twice as long as wild type.
About four to five weeks instead of two to three.
The worms looked normal.
They moved, they fed, they laid eggs.
They were just on a different aging timeline.
This was, when she published it in 1993, an extraordinary result.
A single-gene mutation, doubling lifespan.
It implied that aging was not just damage accumulation.
There was a genetic program — or at least, a genetic dial — that controlled the rate of aging.
Set the dial one way, the worm lives two weeks.
Set it the other way, the worm lives four.
And by changing one gene, you could turn the dial.
The next question was: what did daf-2 encode?
When it was eventually cloned and sequenced, daf-2 turned out to encode the worm homolog of the insulin and insulin-like growth factor receptor.
The worm version of the receptor that, in mammals, binds insulin and IGF-1 and triggers a signaling cascade that controls metabolism, growth, and many other things.
The implication was startling.
The same signaling pathway that, in mammals, controls glucose metabolism — the insulin pathway — controls aging in worms.
And subsequent work showed that the downstream components of the pathway were also conserved.
age-1, another gene whose mutants are long-lived, encodes a PI3-kinase that's activated by daf-2.
Downstream of that sits a transcription factor called daf-16, the worm homolog of the mammalian FOXO transcription factors.
When insulin signaling is active, daf-16 is phosphorylated and held in the cytoplasm.
When insulin signaling is reduced (as in daf-2 mutants), daf-16 moves into the nucleus and turns on a battery of stress-resistance and longevity genes.
The worm lives longer.
The analogy: the insulin pathway is a genetic dial that worms — and possibly we — use to set the pace of aging.
When food is abundant and signaling is high, worms grow fast, reproduce fast, age fast.
When food is scarce or signaling is impaired, worms slow down, become stress-resistant, and live long.
The dial is evolutionary — it lets worms survive bad conditions by waiting them out.
Kenyon's daf-2 mutation is, in effect, a stuck dial: the worm thinks conditions are bad, so it goes into long-lived mode, even though food is fine.
The huge question, when this was first published, was: does the dial exist in mammals?
The answer turned out to be: yes, partially.
Mice with reduced insulin/IGF-1 signaling — mutants in the IGF-1 receptor, mutants in growth-hormone signaling — live longer than wild-type mice.
The Ames dwarf mouse and the Snell dwarf mouse, both of which have impaired growth-hormone signaling and reduced IGF-1, live about 50% longer than normal mice.
The yeast homolog of the same pathway, Sch9, controls aging in yeast.
Human centenarians are enriched for genetic variants in FOXO3 and IGF-1R that reduce insulin signaling activity.
The pathway is conserved.
The dial is real.
In every organism we've looked at — worm, fly, mouse, human — turning down insulin signaling extends lifespan.
The mechanistic detail is fascinating and beyond what we can cover here, but the headline is this: aging has a genetic logic, and that logic is conserved across animals.
Kenyon's worms were the first hint.

The follow-up work, in worm, fly, and mouse, has built up a coherent picture: insulin/IGF-1 signaling, TOR signaling, AMPK, sirtuins — a small set of nutrient-sensing pathways that, together, set the pace at which an animal ages.
Caloric restriction extends lifespan in every organism tested, from yeast to monkeys, because it dials down these pathways.
Drugs like rapamycin and metformin extend lifespan in mice because they inhibit components of these pathways.
Senolytics, the new class of drugs that clear senescent cells, are downstream of the same regulatory logic.

Whether any of this translates into human longevity therapy is still being worked out — there are ongoing clinical trials of metformin for healthspan, of rapamycin analogs for aging-related decline — but the basic biology was nailed in the worm.

The big idea to leave this segment with: the worm gave us the first proof that aging is genetically programmable, and the conservation of that program across animals means worm aging research is a serious candidate for translation to human medicine.
Cynthia Kenyon's worms that live twice as long are not just a curiosity.
They are the leading edge of a research program that might, eventually, give us drugs that extend human healthy lifespan.
Three days per worm generation, two to three weeks per worm lifespan, transparent and cheap — the worm is the platform on which the genetic logic of aging has been worked out.
That is what model organisms do.
Now let's switch organisms.
We've spent five segments on the worm.
The other half of the invertebrate workhorse pair is the fly, and the fly story starts a half-century earlier, with a man named Thomas Hunt Morgan and a fly room at Columbia University.
